critic_raw:
This multi-task learning readmission prediction experiment contains several methodological problems, some of which are self-contradictory within the design.

1. THE EFFICACY AUXILIARY TASK LABEL IS NOT INDEPENDENT OF THE READMISSION LABEL. The efficacy label is 'a composite binary indicator derived from clinical assessments within 14 days post-discharge (resolution of primary diagnosis marker, medication adherence, functional status).' Patients who are readmitted within 30 days will, by definition, have poor post-discharge adherence and functional status — the readmission event itself interrupts the adherence and functional measurement. This means efficacy and readmission are not independent outcomes being jointly learned; efficacy is partially determined by the readmission outcome. Training a multi-task model with correlated labels does not test whether an auxiliary task provides independent regularization — it tests whether the model can learn a redundant signal.

2. TEST SET IS EXPLICITLY USED FOR MODEL SELECTION. Section 7 states: 'Following successful validation, a final configuration verification is conducted on the test set to ensure the model's robustness before it is fully trained on the combined train and validation sets. This step is crucial to confirm that hyperparameter tuning was effective and that the chosen configuration maintains its advantage, which guards against overfitting to a specific development window. After this verification, the test set results guide the decision on whether to train the model on both train and validation data.' This is explicit test-set-based model selection. Any reported test-set metrics are not holdout estimates; they are training objectives. This is the most severe methodological violation in the design.

3. PREPROCESSING FITTED ON COMBINED TRAIN AND VALIDATION SETS RATHER THAN TRAINING ONLY. The design states preprocessing is fit 'on the combined train and validation set before finalizing the model.' This means imputation statistics, scaling, and feature selection are informed by the validation set. While this is less severe than fitting on the full dataset including test, it still contaminates the validation set's independence. Hyperparameter search on the validation set is now implicitly contaminated — the model trained on train data is being evaluated against a validation set whose statistics were used in preprocessing.

4. SAMPLE SIZE FOR THE EFFICACY LABEL IS UNVERIFIED. Efficacy is defined as adherence ≥80% AND ≥75% appointment attendance within 90 days. With 50,000 encounters and substantial missing data (acknowledged as retained with sensitivity analysis), the number of patients with complete efficacy labels is unknown. If efficacy labels are sparse (<10,000 patients), the auxiliary task provides insufficient gradient signal relative to the primary task, and multi-task benefits will be minimal or negative.

5. COMPUTATIONAL EFFICIENCY HYPOTHESIS IS NOT TESTABLE AS STATED. The hypothesis defines 'efficiency' as 'higher discriminative performance with lower model complexity (parameter count, training time).' But the multi-task model has the same shared architecture plus an additional head — it has strictly more parameters than the single-task baseline. The design claims to test whether MTL is 'more efficient,' but the parameter count comparison will always favor the single-task model by construction. Training time comparison will also be confounded by the additional efficacy head computations.
all_issues_raised: Efficacy auxiliary label is partially determined by readmission outcome (readmission interrupts post-discharge adherence measurement); labels are not independent and multi-task learning tests redundant signal
Test set is explicitly used to verify and guide model selection before final training on combined train+validation data; reported test-set metrics are not holdout estimates
Preprocessing fitted on combined train+validation set contaminates validation set independence for hyperparameter search
Sample size with complete efficacy labels (adherence AND appointment attendance within 90 days) is unknown and may be insufficient for auxiliary task gradient signal
Efficiency hypothesis is unstatable because multi-task model has strictly more parameters than single-task baseline by architecture; hypothesis is false by construction
Preprocessing fit on combined train-and-validation set leaks validation-period distribution into preprocessing parameters
Test set used for configuration verification before final training, violating test set independence
Efficacy auxiliary label has non-random missingness concentrated among highest-risk patients, biasing shared encoder
Efficiency claim involves three independent quantities without a prespecified tradeoff rule, making it non-falsifiable
AUROC incorrectly claimed to capture false-negative cost asymmetry; it treats all thresholds equally
Preprocessing fit on combined train and validation set leaks validation-period distribution into training features
Test set is accessed during model development for configuration verification, invalidating it as an unbiased holdout
Efficacy auxiliary task label derived from post-discharge assessments may encode information unavailable at prediction time
Efficiency framing conflates AUROC performance with parameter count, but MTL model has strictly more parameters than the single-task baseline
all_issues_adjudicated: Efficacy auxiliary label is partially determined by readmission outcome (readmission interrupts post-discharge adherence measurement); labels are not independent and multi-task learning tests redundant signal
